request.question: Who wrote the book that Peggy Robertson's 1948 movie is based on?

title: Whispering Smith (TV series)
Under Capricorn
Anna Robertson Brown Lindsay
Cliff Robertson
Instauration
Peggy Robertson
W. Bruce Cameron
Joseph Knight (novel)
A Dog's Purpose (film)
Kirk Alyn
sentences: Whispering Smith is an American Western series that originally aired on NBC.
It has the same ultimate source material as the 1948 movie of the same title (and some other films), but differs in some significant respects.

Under Capricorn is a 1949 British historical thriller film directed by Alfred Hitchcock about a couple in Australia who started out as lady and stable boy in Ireland, and who are now bound together by a horrible secret.
The film was based on the novel "Under Capricorn" (1937) by Helen Simpson, with a screenplay by James Bridie.
It was adapted to the screen by Hume Cronyn.
This was Hitchcock's second film in Technicolor, and like the preceding color film "Rope" (1948), it also featured 10-minute takes.
Anna Robertson Brown Lindsay (1864–1948) was the first woman to earn a doctorate at the University of Pennsylvania.
She wrote a number of books on theological topics, most of which were published in the early-1900s.
Anna Robertson Brown Lindsay was the daughter of a Presbyterian minister and the first woman to graduate with a Ph.D in English from the University of Pennsylvania.
Clifford Parker Robertson III (September 9, 1923 – September 10, 2011) was an American actor with a film and television career that spanned half a century.
Robertson portrayed a young John F. Kennedy in the 1963 film "PT 109", and won the 1968 Academy Award for Best Actor for his role in the movie "Charly".

On television, he portrayed retired astronaut Buzz Aldrin in the 1976 adaptation of Aldrin's autobiographic "Return to Earth", played a fictional character based on Director of Central Intelligence Richard Helms in the 1977 miniseries "", and portrayed Henry Ford in the 1987 "Ford: The Man and the Machine".
His last well-known film appearances were in 2002 through 2007 as Uncle Ben in the "Spider-Man" film trilogy.
Instauration was a monthly magazine published from December 1975 through February 2000, ending with volume 25, issue 3.
It was published by Wilmot Robertson (Sumner Humphrey Ireland), who also wrote "The Dispossessed Majority" (1973) and "The Ethnostate" (1992).
"The Dispossessed Majority" was decried as a white supremacist book when notice of it was mailed to students at the Marist School and the Lovett School in 1987.
The magazine and Robertson's books share much in common.
The editorial office of Instauration was in Cape Canaveral, Florida, and the publisher was Howard Allen Enterprises, Inc.

Margaret "Peggy" Robertson (née Singer; 13 September 1916 6 February 1998) was a British-born script supervisor and personal assistant to Alfred Hitchcock from the 1940s through the 1970s during which time she worked on the films "Under Capricorn" (1948) and "Stage Fright" (1950), "Vertigo" (1958), "Psycho" (1960), "The Birds" (1963), and all of the director's remaining films.
William Bruce Cameron (born 1960 in Petoskey, Michigan) is an American author, columnist, and humorist.
Cameron is most famous for his novel "A Dog's Purpose", which spent 52 weeks on the "New York Times" bestseller list and is the first book in a two book series that concludes with "A Dog's Journey".

The book is the basis for the movie version starring Dennis Quaid, Britt Robertson, Peggy Lipton, K.J. Apa, Juliet Rylance, Luke Kirby, John Ortiz and Pooch Hall, an Amblin Entertainment/Reliance Entertainment/Walden Media release in theaters January 27, 2017.
Joseph Knight is a historical novel by Scottish author James Robertson published in 2003 by Fourth Estate.
It was the Saltire Society Scottish Book of the Year in 2003 and won the Scottish Arts Council Book of the Year Award in 2004.

The novel is based on the true story of a slave brought from the Jamaica to Scotland, and the novel revolves primarily around the cities of Dundee, near where Robertson was then living, and Edinburgh.

A Dog's Purpose is a 2017 American comedy-drama film directed by Lasse Hallström and written by W. Bruce Cameron, Cathryn Michon, Audrey Wells, Maya Forbes and Wally Wolodarsky, based on the 2010 novel of the same name by Cameron.
The film stars Britt Robertson, KJ Apa, Juliet Rylance, John Ortiz, Kirby Howell-Baptiste, Peggy Lipton, Dennis Quaid and Josh Gad.

Kirk Alyn (born John Feggo Jr., October 8, 1910 – March 14, 1999) was an American actor, best known for being the first actor to play the DC Comics character Superman in live-action for the 1948 movie serial "Superman" and its 1950 sequel "Atom Man vs. Superman", as well as Blackhawk from the "Blackhawk" movie serial in 1952, and General Sam Lane in 1978's "Superman: The Movie".

candidate.summarize1.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `passages`, produce the fields `summary`.

Task: Compress the grounded first-hop evidence into a single concise, retrieval-oriented intermediate state to guide the next-hop retrieval. Do not produce the final answer or perform any new retrieval here.

Required content of `summary` (single field):
- A short header identifying the primary topic(s) found in the passages (1 line).
- A concise enumeration of the concrete facts supported verbatim by the provided passages: list entity mentions and directly supported factual spans (dates, years, titles, roles). Use very short quoted fragments (<= ~15 words) only when those words appear verbatim in the passages. Keep to facts present in the passages; do not add or infer new facts.
- Explicitly expose any ambiguity or missing information that blocks answering: if multiple candidate entities or page variants appear, enumerate those candidates and label the name as ambiguous; if a required target fact is missing or ambiguous, label it as the missing fact and identify the bridge entity that connects this hop to the next.
- Prioritized next-hop retrieval directives (1–4 items): concrete page titles or disambiguation pages to fetch, explicit article sections to inspect (e.g., lede/infobox, "Career", "Publication", "History"), or tight search keywords. Order them by priority and be specific (include possible disambiguation terms or section names).

Formatting and style constraints:
- Keep the entire `summary` concise: a short header plus 1–4 brief sentences or 2–6 short bullet lines in total.
- Preserve the habit of extracting and citing concrete facts verbatim from the passages (names, dates, passage labels).
- Do not output a final answer, comparison, or any numeric fact that purports to resolve the question here. Even if the passages fully and unambiguously contain all required facts, present that evidence and explicitly state "no additional retrieval needed," but defer producing any final answer to a downstream component.
- If a name or term has multiple distinct variants in the passages, do not pick one without disambiguating evidence; enumerate candidates instead.
- Do not invent or add facts not present in the supplied passages. Avoid long verbatim quotations; prefer short extracts (<= ~15 words) or paraphrase.

Output expectations: produce exactly one field `summary` containing the concise, retrieval-oriented summary that meets the above requirements.
candidate.create_query_hop2.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `summary_1`, produce the fields `query`.

Produce exactly one compact, retrieval-focused second-hop query in the field `query`. Use only the provided `question` and `summary_1` as inputs. Do not include any explanation, answer, fallback text, lists, or additional fields — the output must be a single one-line query string.

Decision templates (choose the single template that matches the missing evidence implied by summary_1):
- Connector between two named items: emit a short interrogative naming both items and requesting the linking evidence (e.g., artist album recording) in one line. Retain the visible item tokens from the inputs.
- Metadata for a single anchor title: emit a compact keyword-style query consisting of the anchor plus brief attribute keywords (for example: artist genre).
- Temporal or aggregate fact: include the year or season token plus the entity type and a short cue such as season count.

Strict formatting rules:
- Emit exactly one line and exactly one string in `query` containing only the query text.
- Keep the query compact (aim for ≤ 10–12 words/tokens) and free of punctuation or explanatory clauses.
- Preserve visible tokens from `question` and `summary_1` (title words, years, or entity names) to anchor retrieval.
- Do not answer, conclude, enumerate candidates, assert hidden facts, or include user-facing instructions.

Produce the `query` field now.
candidate.summarize2.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `context`, `passages`, produce the fields `summary`.

Instructions (must follow exactly):
- Emit a single field named `summary` containing only 1–3 concise sentences that integrate the first-hop `context` and the second-hop `passages` into a grounded post-retrieval state.
- If the `context` already contains a concise answer sentence, keep that sentence as the first sentence of `summary` unless the `passages` clearly contradict it; append supporting sentence(s) after it rather than replacing it.
- Preserve the primary answer name(s) exactly as they appear in the inputs (context or passages) unless those names clearly conflict with stronger passage content.
- When one or more `passages` supply short biographical, membership, partnership, or corroborating facts about a named entity from the `context`, append at most one brief supporting sentence per relevant named person. Each such supporting sentence should:
  - begin with the entity's canonical name as shown in the passages (if available),
  - paraphrase up to one or two key attributes present in the passages (examples: nationality, award/winner status, current affiliation/team, partner identity, founding/formation fact, birth date), and
  - avoid long verbatim copying (prefer paraphrase; use short quotes only when a brief exact phrase from a passage is necessary for clarity).
- If the question requires naming both members of a partnership or collaborator pair, name both people in the summary and, if passages provide partner-specific attributes, include one brief attribute for each as separate short clauses or sentences if needed.
- Do not invent facts not present in the provided passages. If passages conflict on a specific attribute, do not synthesize a new reconciled fact; instead prefer the passage that most directly addresses the question or omit the disputed attribute (or state necessary ambiguity only when needed to avoid a false claim).
- Keep the `summary` compact and focused: avoid extraneous biography, discography/chart data, or unrelated commentary. When `context` is absent or is not a concise answer, produce a 1–2 sentence integrated summary naming the relevant entity(ies) and including at most one brief paraphrased attribute drawn from the passages.

Output requirement: exactly one JSON field `summary` whose value is the integrated 1–3 sentence summary.
candidate.final_answer.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `summary_1`, `summary_2`, produce the fields `answer`.

Instructions:
- Use only `summary_1` and `summary_2` as the evidential basis for the final decision; do not add new facts or external information.
- First determine whether the question is a binary (yes/no) proposition and whether both `summary_1` and `summary_2` clearly and unambiguously support the same binary outcome.
- If BOTH summaries unambiguously assert the same binary conclusion, emit exactly one lowercase token as the entire content of `answer`: use "yes" for an affirmative conclusion and "no" for a negative conclusion. The `answer` field must contain that single token and nothing else (no punctuation, no extra whitespace, no explanation).
- Prefer `summary_2` as the decisive statement when it clarifies or restates `summary_1`, but still require that both summaries support the same binary judgment before emitting the single-token `answer`.
- If the summaries conflict, if either summary hedges, is ambiguous, silent about the binary relation, or otherwise fails to clearly agree, do NOT apply the single-token rule; instead produce the module's normal fallback/unresolved output for `answer`.
- Always remain truthful to the visible summaries and do not alter their factual conclusions.

She wrote a number of books on theological topics, most of which were published in the early-1900s.  Anna Robertson Brown Lindsay was the daughter of a Presbyterian minister and the first woman to graduate with a Ph.D in English from the University of Pennsylvania.

Pichilemu Blues | Pichilemu Blues is a 1993 book written by Chilean politician Esteban Valenzuela.  A movie based on the book was also released, starring Peggy Cordero, Ximena Nogueira and Evaristo Acevedo.

W. Bruce Cameron | William Bruce Cameron (born 1960 in Petoskey, Michigan) is an American author, columnist, and humorist.  Cameron is most famous for his novel "A Dog's Purpose", which spent 52 weeks on the "New York Times" bestseller list and is the first book in a two book series that concludes with "A Dog's Journey". Al Hine | Al Hine (1915–1974) was a reporter, novelist, and movie producer who wrote numerous books including "Lord Love a Duck", which was made into a movie starring Tuesday Weld and Roddy McDowall, and pop novels based on the "Bewitched" TV series and the Beatles' movie "Help!" .

Peggie Castle | Peggie Castle (December 22, 1927 – August 11, 1973) was an American actress who specialized in playing the "other woman" in B-movies.  She was also billed under the names Peggy Castle and Peggie Call.

Edward McNally (NFL) | Edward "Skipper" McNally (June 20, 1923 – February 20, 1987), in the world of NFL, was a legendary gatecrasher of Super Bowls in the 1960s, 1970s, and 1980s.  He is from Boston, (North Quincy) and has had bit parts in movies including "On the Waterfront" with Marlon Brando and "Charly", the movie based on the book "Flowers for Algernon", with Cliff Robertson.
response.summary_1:
Primary topic: Peggy Robertson's work on the 1948 film "Under Capricorn".

- Peggy Robertson: "Margaret 'Peggy' Robertson (née Singer; 13 September 1916 6 February 1998)" (passage [1]).
- Role/context: she "worked on the films 'Under Capricorn' (1948)..." and was "personal assistant to Alfred Hitchcock" (passage [1]).
- Missing fact (blocks answer): the passages do not state who "wrote the book" that the 1948 film "Under Capricorn" is based on — the author of the source novel is absent. Bridge entity: "Under Capricorn (1948 film)".

Prioritized next-hop retrievals:
1. "Under Capricorn (film)" — fetch lede/infobox and the "Based on"/"Source material" or "Production" sections.
2. "Under Capricorn (novel)" or "Under Capricorn" (novel) page — lede/author information.
3. Search keywords if pages not found: "Under Capricorn novel author" and "Under Capricorn based on novel who wrote".
hop2_docs: Under Capricorn | Under Capricorn is a 1949 British historical thriller film directed by Alfred Hitchcock about a couple in Australia who started out as lady and stable boy in Ireland, and who are now bound together by a horrible secret.  The film was based on the novel "Under Capricorn" (1937) by Helen Simpson, with a screenplay by James Bridie.  It was adapted to the screen by Hume Cronyn.  This was Hitchcock's second film in Technicolor, and like the preceding color film "Rope" (1948), it also featured 10-minute takes.
Under Capricorn (novel) | Under Capricorn is a 1937 historical novel by Helen Simpson.
Under Capricorn (miniseries) | Under Capricorn is a 1983 Australian miniseries based on the novel by Helen Simpson.

Capricorn (astrology) | Capricorn (♑) is the tenth astrological sign in the zodiac, originating from the constellation of Capricornus.  It spans the 270–300th degree of the zodiac, corresponding to celestial longitude.  Under the tropical zodiac, the sun transits this area from December 22 to January 19 each year, and under the sidereal zodiac, the sun transits the constellation of Capricorn from approximately January 16 to February 16.  In astrology, Capricorn is considered an earth sign, negative sign, and one of the four cardinal signs.  Capricorn is said to be ruled by the planet Saturn.

Douglas Anne Munson | Douglas Anne Munson (February 17, 1948 – December 22, 2003) was an attorney, teacher, and author of four critically acclaimed novels.  She published three of those novels under the pseudonym of Mercedes Lambert.

Tropic of Capricorn (novel) | Tropic of Capricorn is a semi-autobiographical novel by Henry Miller, first published by Obelisk Press in Paris in 1939.  A prequel of sorts to Miller's first published novel, 1934's "Tropic of Cancer", it was banned in the United States until a 1961 Justice Department ruling declared that its contents were not obscene.

Sandra Brown | Sandra Lynn Brown (born March 12, 1948) is an American bestselling author of romantic novels and thriller suspense novels.  Brown has also published works under the pen names of Rachel Ryan, Laura Jordan, and Erin St. Claire.
response.summary_2: Peggy Robertson's 1948 film Under Capricorn was based on a novel written by Helen Simpson. Helen Simpson is the author of the 1937 historical novel Under Capricorn.
